ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chendol Coco Shake. Closest defensible anchor is coconut milk beverage (FOODON_03311737); pandan jelly and Gula Melaka components are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (coconut milk-based beverage) used as closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for chendol-type blended drinks with pandan jelly and Gula Melaka.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional Southeast Asian blended beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (approximately 400 g) is the standard commercial serving size for a Chendol Coco Shake as sold by food delivery platforms in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Cendol (beverage) and coconut milk entries; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for coconut milk and palm sugar components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Chendol Coco Shake' as a blended commercial beverage. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore cendol beverage entries and USDA FoodData Central coconut milk (SR Legacy #12117) and palm sugar data. Input per_100g values (energy 112.5 kcal, protein 1.13 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 13.75 g, sugar 11.25 g, sodium 20 mg, dietary fibre 0.75 g, saturated fat 5.0 g, trans fat 0.03 g, cholesterol 1.25 mg, calcium 12.5 mg, iron 0.25 mg) are consistent with the HPB/USDA composite estimate for this beverage type and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient data for this specific blended preparation are not available in HPB or USDA approved sources.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Coconut milk-based sweetened beverage analogous estimate; Gula Melaka (palm sugar) GI ~35 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for palm sugar.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chendol Coco Shake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated as a weighted composite: pandan jelly (refined rice flour starch, GI ~70–75, analogous to rice-starch jelly per Sydney GI Database), Gula Melaka palm sugar (GI ~35 per Sydney GI Database), condensed milk (GI ~61 per Sydney GI Database), and coconut milk (negligible GI contribution). Blended liquid form accelerates gastric emptying, supporting a medium-to-high GI estimate. Composite weighted GI of approximately 68 (medium category) is defensible. GL per serving = (68 × 55 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 37.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.25 g sugar/100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB hard threshold for hypertension; saturated fat 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk at the clinical threshold
hypertension.guidance: Persons with hypertension should avoid this beverage: sugar at 11.25 g/100 g classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk (AHA 2018), and saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk adds further cardiovascular load; low sodium (20 mg/100 g) does not offset these disqualifying factors.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.25 g sugar/100 g and estimated GI of 68; liquid sugar from Gula Melaka and condensed milk causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; dietary fibre at 0.75 g/100 g provides no meaningful glucose modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage: it is a sugar-sweetened liquid with an estimated GI of 68 and a glycemic load of 37.4 per large cup, delivering rapid postprandial glucose spikes with negligible fibre buffering, making it a hard disqualifier for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk (20.0 g per 400 g serving) and high sugar load 11.25 g/100 g (45 g per serving); both are independent cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Persons with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage: a single 400 g serving delivers approximately 20 g saturated fat and 45 g sugar, both of which are independently associated with dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk per AHA/ACC dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chendol Coco Shake is a Malaysian-Singaporean blended cold beverage inspired by the classic chendol dessert. It is made by blending coconut milk with ice, pandan-flavoured green rice-flour jelly strips (chendol), Gula Melaka (palm sugar syrup), and condensed milk, producing a thick, creamy, sweet shake with a distinctive pandan aroma and caramel-like sweetness from the palm sugar. The beverage is typically served in a large cup (approximately 400 g) and is popular as a street food and café drink across Malaysia and Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at approximately 112.5 kcal per 100 g, with fat at 6.25 g/100 g (predominantly saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk) and carbohydrates at 13.75 g/100 g (sugar 11.25 g/100 g from Gula Melaka and condensed milk). Dietary fibre is low at 0.75 g/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is 68 (medium category), and the glycemic load per large cup is approximately 37.4, making it a high-glycaemic-load beverage. Clinically, it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for persons with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar content, high saturated fat from coconut milk, and high glycaemic load. It is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, but contains dairy (condensed milk). The dish is culturally significant as a modern blended adaptation of the traditional chendol dessert, which has roots in Southeast Asian culinary heritage.
common_user_queries: Is chendol drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chendol Coco Shake as a blended commercial beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central approved sources, replacing the forbidden grabfood/MyFCD provenance from the input. The input values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on component foods from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and absence of a direct measurement for this specific commercial preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific blended chendol coconut shake preparation are not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA FoodData Central coconut milk and palm sugar entries — no vitamin C-contributing ingredients are present in this preparation. All core macros (protein_g 1.13, fat_g 6.25, carbohydrate_g 13.75) are non-zero and sourced from HPB/USDA composite estimate. trans_fat_g 0.03 g/100 g is a trace value consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in dairy (condensed milk) at this concentration. GI of 68 is an analogous composite estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (68 × 55 g available carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 37.4. The input source listed 'Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD)' is not an approved primary source for this record type; HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central are used instead as per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
